content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 12 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.1 — "It is necessary to boast" vs. "I must go on boasting" vs. "This boasting will do no good, but I must go on"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It is necessary to boast — [though it is] not profitable" | Literal; two clauses |
| LOGOS | "I must go on boasting. Though there is nothing to be gained by it" | Two sentences |
| PNEUMA | "This boasting will do no good, but I must go on. I will reluctantly tell about visions" | Adds "reluctantly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "reluctantly" — not in the Greek. Paul signals that boasting is unprofitable but necessary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds an adverb.

---

### v.2 — Third-person reference: "a person in Christos" vs. "a man in Christ" vs. "I was caught up"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I know a person in Christos" | anthropon as gender-neutral "person" |
| LOGOS | "I know a man in Christ" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "I was caught up to the third heaven" | First person; drops third-person device entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's use of the third person ("I know a man") is a deliberate rhetorical device — he distances himself from the experience. PNEUMA's switch to first person ("I was caught up") destroys this literary strategy.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Paul's third-person self-reference is a deliberate rhetorical and theological choice — he separates the visionary from the suffering apostle. PNEUMA's first-person rendering eliminates this crucial distinction.

---

### v.4 — "unspeakable words" vs. "things that cannot be told" vs. "things so astounding"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "heard unspeakable words which it is not lawful for a person to speak" | arreta rhemata as "unspeakable words" |
| LOGOS | "heard things that cannot be told, which man may not utter" | "Things" for rhemata; "may not utter" |
| PNEUMA | "heard things so astounding that they cannot be expressed in words, things no human is allowed to tell" | Adds "astounding"; two clauses |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. arreta means "unutterable/inexpressible" — not "astounding" (which is an evaluation PNEUMA adds). The prohibition is about the nature of the words, not their impressiveness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds evaluative content.

---

### v.7 — "thorn in the flesh" vs. "thorn in my flesh" / "strike me" vs. "harass me" vs. "torment me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satanas, that it might strike me" | skolops as "thorn"; kolaphize as "strike/buffet" |
| LOGOS | "a thorn was given me in the flesh, a messenger of Satan to harass me" | "Harass" for kolaphizo |
| PNEUMA | "a thorn in my flesh, a messenger from Satan to torment me" | "Torment" for kolaphizo; "from" for genitive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kolaphizo means "to strike with the fist/buffet" — "harass" captures the ongoing nature. PNEUMA's "torment" is too intense; EMET's "strike" is too punctual. The genitive "of Satan" (not "from Satan") identifies the messenger as Satan's agent.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Degree of affliction differs.

---

### v.9 — "Sufficient for you is the grace of me" vs. "My grace is sufficient for you" vs. "My grace is all you need"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Sufficient for you [is] the grace of me; for the power is perfected in weakness" | Preserves Greek word order (predicate first) |
| LOGOS | "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness" | English word order; "made perfect" |
| PNEUMA | "My grace is all you need. My power works best in weakness" | "All you need" for arkei; "works best" for teleitai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "all you need" slightly narrows arkei ("is sufficient/is enough"). More significantly, "works best" for teleitai ("is perfected/completed/brought to its goal") changes the meaning: Paul's point is not that power functions optimally in weakness but that it reaches its telos — its completion and perfection — in weakness. "Made perfect" preserves this teleological sense.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. teleitai ("is perfected/completed") vs. "works best" — the difference between teleological completion and mere optimization. "Made perfect" preserves the deeper theological claim.

---

### v.10 — "powerful" vs. "strong" vs. "strong"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "when I am weak, then I am powerful" | dynatos as "powerful" |
| LOGOS | "when I am weak, then I am strong" | "Strong" — idiomatic English |
| PNEUMA | "when I am weak, then I am strong" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA. dynatos can mean "powerful" or "strong." "Strong" is the more natural English and pairs better with "weak" (both are simpler, physical-sounding words).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor word choice.

---

### v.12 — "signs of the apostle" vs. "signs of a true apostle" vs. "proof that I am a true apostle"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the signs of the apostle were worked among you in all endurance" | ta semeia tou apostolou — definite article |
| LOGOS | "The signs of a true apostle were performed among you with utmost patience" | Adds "true"; "patience" for hypomone |
| PNEUMA | "I certainly gave you proof that I am a true apostle. For I patiently did many signs and wonders" | First person; "proof"; restructured |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "True" is implicit in the phrase. PNEUMA restructures entirely and adds "certainly" and "I patiently did" — Paul uses the passive voice ("were performed"), maintaining that God is the agent.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA shifts from passive (God acts) to active (Paul acts).

---

### v.16 — "I was crafty, you say" vs. "But granting that" vs. "others still think"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being crafty, I took you by guile!" | Paul's words; no quotation framing |
| LOGOS | "I was crafty, you say, and got the better of you by deceit" | "You say" — attributes to opponents |
| PNEUMA | "Some of you admit I was not a burden to you. But others still think I was sneaky and took advantage of you by trickery" | Adds "some of you...others" — division not in text |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA invents a division in the church ("some...others") not present in the text. Paul is quoting an accusation against him — LOGOS's "you say" captures this.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA invents a church division.

---

### v.19 — "building up" vs. "upbuilding" vs. "strengthen"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the building up of you" | oikodome as "building up" |
| LOGOS | "all for your upbuilding" | Same concept; noun form |
| PNEUMA | "Everything we do, dear friends, is to strengthen you" | "Strengthen" for oikodome |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. oikodome ("building up/edification") is a key Pauline architectural metaphor. PNEUMA's "strengthen" loses the building imagery that runs throughout the letter (cf. 10:8, 13:10).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA drops a running architectural metaphor.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 3 | "Reluctantly," "astounding," "certainly" |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | First person for third person (v.2), active for passive (v.12), invented division (v.16) |
| PNEUMA theological shifts | 1 | "Works best" vs. "made perfect" — optimization vs. teleological completion |
| PNEUMA metaphor loss | 1 | "Strengthen" for oikodome — drops building imagery |
| Degree of affliction | 1 | "Torment" vs. "harass" vs. "strike" for kolaphizo |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 12 contains the theological heart of the letter, and the disagreements are especially consequential. PNEUMA's shift from third to first person in the paradise vision (v.2) eliminates Paul's deliberate rhetorical distance. The rendering of teleitai as "works best" rather than "made perfect" (v.9) changes the theology from teleological completion to mere optimization. The consensus preserves Paul's literary strategies and theological precision.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
